brendler2006.author: Brendler, T., Gruenwald, J., Ulbricht, C. and Basch, E.
brendler2006.title: Devil's Claw (Harpagophytum procumbens DC): an evidence-based systematic review by the Natural Standard Research Collaboration

chrubasik2004.author: Chrubasik, S.
chrubasik2004.title: Devil's claw extract as an example of the effectiveness of herbal analgesics

actions.action: Anti-inflammatory and analgesic for osteoarthritis (hip and knee) pain
Relief of chronic non-specific low back pain
General joint pain and arthritis support

constituents.name: Iridoid glycosides (harpagoside, harpagide, procumbide)
constituents.note: Harpagoside is the main marker compound and anti-inflammatory principle; effective extracts are standardised to it.

contraindications.note: Because it is a bitter that increases stomach-acid and bile secretion, it is contraindicated in active peptic ulcer, gastritis and (without medical supervision) gallstones.
May add to the effect of anticoagulants (e.g. warfarin) and may influence blood sugar, blood pressure and heart rhythm; use caution with these medicines.
Avoid during pregnancy (oxytocic/uterine effect) and while breastfeeding.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific literature (Natural Standard evidence-based systematic review; Der Orthopade review of herbal analgesia). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (pain, arthritis, back-pain, inflammation); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

kenny2022.author: Kenny, C.R., Stojakowska, A., Furey, A. and Lucey, B.
kenny2022.title: From Monographs to Chromatograms: The Antimicrobial Potential of Inula helenium L. (Elecampane) Naturalised in Ireland

actions.action: Expectorant for productive cough and bronchitis (long-standing respiratory remedy)
Antimicrobial, notably anti-staphylococcal (supports respiratory and skin infection)

constituents.name: Sesquiterpene lactones (alantolactone, isoalantolactone, igalan)
constituents.note: The antimicrobial principles of the root.

constituents.name: Inulin
constituents.note: A fructan polysaccharide abundant in the root (the genus Inula gives inulin its name).

contraindications.note: The sesquiterpene lactones (especially alantolactone) are known skin sensitisers and can cause allergic contact dermatitis; people sensitive to the daisy family (Asteraceae) should be cautious.
Large doses can cause nausea, vomiting and diarrhoea; safety in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not established, so avoid medicinal doses.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific literature (Molecules antimicrobial/ethnobotanical study). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (cough, bronchitis, infection); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

sharifi2024.author: Sharifi, M., Nourani, N., Sanaie, S. and Hamedeyazdan, S.
sharifi2024.title: The effect of Oenothera biennis (Evening primrose) oil on inflammatory diseases: a systematic review of clinical trials

bamford2013.author: Bamford, J.T.M., Ray, S., Musekiwa, A., van Gool, C., Humphreys, R. and Ernst, E.
bamford2013.title: Oral evening primrose oil and borage oil for eczema

actions.action: Dietary source of gamma-linolenic acid (GLA), an omega-6 fatty acid (the rationale for its use)
Eczema / atopic dermatitis - popularly used, but a Cochrane review found oral EPO is NOT effective
Premenstrual syndrome and cyclical breast pain - some positive but inconsistent trial results
Menopausal hot flushes - some trial benefit reported
Skin hydration and barrier function in healthy skin

constituents.name: Gamma-linolenic acid (GLA) and linoleic acid
constituents.note: Omega-6 essential fatty acids of the seed oil; GLA is the constituent the supplement is taken for.

constituents.name: Other seed-oil fatty acids and plant sterols

contraindications.note: Evidence is weak and largely negative: a Cochrane review concluded that oral evening primrose oil is not an effective treatment for eczema, and the broader trial literature is heterogeneous. Adverse effects are mild and mainly gastrointestinal.
May increase bleeding risk and has been reported to interact with warfarin; avoid concentrated/medicinal use in pregnancy unless advised by a clinician.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific literature (BMC Complementary Medicine and Therapies systematic review; Cochrane review of EPO/borage oil for eczema). Evidence for most symptom claims is weak or negative, reflected in needs-review action statuses. Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (eczema, pms, skin-irritation, inflammation, menopause); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

paduch2014.author: Paduch, R. and colleagues
paduch2014.title: Assessment of Eyebright (Euphrasia officinalis L.) Extract Activity in Relation to Human Corneal Cells Using In Vitro Tests

actions.action: Astringent and anti-inflammatory for eye irritation, redness and conjunctivitis (traditional, as an eye compress or bath)
Eases eye strain and watery, itchy eyes from allergy (hay fever / allergic conjunctivitis)
Soothes upper-respiratory catarrh and mild infection (traditional)

constituents.name: Iridoid glycosides (notably aucubin)
constituents.note: Give the plant its anti-inflammatory action on irritated tissue.

constituents.name: Tannins
constituents.note: Astringent constituents useful in watery, irritated eyes.

contraindications.note: CRITICAL: do not put non-sterile, homemade eyebright preparations directly into the eye - this risks serious eye infection. Use only commercially prepared sterile products in the eye, or apply a strained, cooled infusion as a compress over closed eyelids.
Traditional use only; persistent eye symptoms, pain, discharge or vision change need assessment by an eye-care professional. Safety in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not established.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific and general literature (in-vitro corneal-cell study; Herbal Reality monograph). Euphrasia is now placed in Orobanchaceae (formerly Scrophulariaceae). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (eye-strain, allergy, infection); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited. NOTE sterility caution for eye use.

wider2015.author: Wider, B., Pittler, M.H. and Ernst, E.
wider2015.title: Feverfew for preventing migraine

actions.action: Migraine prophylaxis - reduces attack frequency (evidence favourable for some standardised preparations, though mixed)
Traditional use for fever and for joint pain / arthritis

constituents.name: Sesquiterpene lactones (parthenolide)
constituents.note: Parthenolide is the principal active compound, to which extracts are often standardised.

contraindications.note: As a member of the daisy family (Asteraceae), it can cause allergic reactions in people sensitive to ragweed and related plants. Chewing fresh leaves commonly causes mouth ulcers and oral soreness.

Stopping feverfew abruptly after prolonged use can cause a 'post-feverfew' rebound of headache, anxiety and muscle/joint stiffness. Avoid in pregnancy (uterine-stimulant) and breastfeeding, and use caution with anticoagulant/antiplatelet medicines.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific literature (Cochrane systematic review of feverfew for migraine prevention). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (headache, migraine, inflammation, fever, arthritis); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.
